narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model estimated a 47% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. The primary driver pushing the prediction toward failure was your LSAT score of 24, well below the dataset average of 36.70, making it the most influential factor in the model's decision for you. This low score signaled a higher risk of struggling with the rigorous demands of law school and the bar, outweighing other strengths in your profile. On a more positive note, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 7—above the average of 5.53—strongly pulled the prediction toward success, showing your academic performance improved notably later in your studies. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 7, higher than the 5.71 average, provided a helpful boost, indicating consistent potential despite early challenges. Your middle family income bracket and full-time student status, which is the norm for over 92% of applicants, played lesser roles but aligned with typical profiles. Compared to other students, your high school deciles stand out as better than average, suggesting you have the grit to compete academically, but the LSAT gap was too wide for the model to overlook. This isn't a judgment on your overall worth or potential as a lawyer—many successful professionals overcome similar hurdles through targeted preparation. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the 36.70 average, as even a modest improvement could flip the prediction in your favor. Consider supplementing with bar prep resources or academic coaching to build on your strong decile rankings. Reapplying with that boost, perhaps to schools with more holistic reviews, could open doors—your profile shows real promise beneath the surface. Keep pushing; persistence like yours is what defines bar passers.
